Ob/Gyn Care Integration Boosts Mental Health Access

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Catherine Monk
5mo
  • Report this post

What happens when insurance-based mental health care is built into Ob/Gyn care?  ⠀ In our new study, we examined access to treatment for Women’s Mental Health @Ob/Gyn, an insurance-based mental health service embedded within Ob/Gyn practices.  ⠀ Analyzing nearly three years of data from more than 2,600 women, we asked: Does integration combined with accepting insurance improve access and sustained engagement?  ⠀ Key findings:  ⠀ • 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐞𝐝: 65% of referred women initiated care—well above rates reported in comparable programs.  ⠀ • 𝐌𝐞𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐟𝐮𝐥 𝐬𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭: Over one-third attended three or more visits, with the highest likelihood of sustained engagement among pregnant, postpartum, and women who had experienced miscarriage or stillbirth.  ⠀ • 𝐁𝐫𝐨𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐜𝐡: Nearly 40% of those who initiated care were neither pregnant nor trying to conceive, underscoring unmet mental health needs across the Ob/Gyn lifespan.  ⠀ • 𝐏𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐥 𝐛𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐫𝐬: Gaps in insurance coverage—particularly plans that cover physical but outsource for mental health care—significantly limited sustained engagement.  ⠀ The takeaway is clear: embedding insurance-based mental health into Ob/Gyn care is both feasible and impactful. At the same time, barriers in coverage continue to constrain the full potential of behavioral health integration.  ⠀ I’m grateful to our clinical teams, collaborators, and funders who made this work possible. We hope these findings contribute to policy and systems changes that honor the WHO’s adage: there is no health without mental health.  ⠀ 🔗 Full article: https://lnkd.in/ejddKthS 

Mental health service utilization in a novel insurance-based Ob/Gyn integrated model for women across the lifespan - Archives of Women's Mental Health link.springer.com

    Impact of Ghost Networks on Psychiatrists: Fighting Back Most of us have family members or friends who have experienced the frustration of navigating insurance provider networks—only to encounter listings of physicians who are not accepting new patients, have moved, retired, or are no longer participating in the network. This phenomenon, known as a “ghost network,” has been a persistent problem for years and is particularly pronounced in psychiatry compared with many other specialties. Historically, attention has focused on the harm to patients who are unable to access timely care. In 2023, I represented the American Psychiatric Association (APA) in testimony before the U.S. Senate Finance Committee on this issue. More recently, the focus has begun to broaden—from the impact on patients alone to the harm these practices cause to psychiatrists themselves. The APA has now filed a lawsuit against a large New York State health plan, alleging that inaccurate network listings damage the professional reputations of psychiatrists who are falsely identified as in-network providers and erode the trust our patients place in us. This important dimension of the problem has received far less attention in the past and may provide additional leverage to hold insurers accountable for maintaining accurate, up-to-date provider directories. Progress! https://lnkd.in/ejyXRSPd

    Mental Health Parity updates: a long-term win for treatment providers and patients. Recent updates and enforcement around the **Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA)** are creating real change across commercial insurance — and while implementation hasn’t been seamless, the direction is undeniably positive for behavioral health providers. What parity enforcement is *meant* to do (and increasingly *is* doing): • Align **behavioral health coverage** with medical/surgical benefits — not just on paper, but operationally • Reduce excessive **utilization management and prior authorization barriers** for MH/SUD care • Expand recognition and coverage of **levels of care** like detox, residential, PHP, and IOP • Support **fairer reimbursement structures** that reflect clinical intensity and provider costs • Improve **network adequacy**, access, and continuity of care for patients For treatment facilities and clinicians, stronger parity enforcement means: ✔️ Fewer arbitrary denials ✔️ More clinically driven care decisions ✔️ Greater financial sustainability ✔️ Increased payer accountability While system updates and growing pains are expected as payers adjust policies and adjudication logic, parity enforcement represents a critical shift toward **treating behavioral health as essential healthcare — not an exception**. Curious how others are experiencing these changes: * Are you seeing improvements in access or approvals? * Has parity enforcement helped conversations with payers? * Where does implementation still fall short? Important progress is happening — and providers should stay engaged, informed, and vocal as parity continues to evolve. #MentalHealthParity #BehavioralHealth #SubstanceUseDisorder #HealthcarePolicy #TreatmentProviders #ValueBasedCare #HealthcareLeadership #AccessToCare

    What happens when access to psychotherapy is expanded? 🔹More people receive psychotherapy 🔹Suicide attempts decline 🔹Total public health insurance costs decrease, because the use of other health-care services drops by a lot These are among the key results of a paper by Benjamin Ly Serena studying the causal effects of expanding access to psychotherapy in Denmark in 2008. Here's the Abstract of the paper: "This paper estimates the causal effect of scaling up access to psychotherapy. I study a 2008 reform of the Danish public health insurance, which introduced 60% coverage for psychotherapy by private practice psychologists for patients aged 18–37 diagnosed with depression or anxiety. Using administrative data from 1995–2019 and quasi-experimental methods, I show that psychotherapy coverage reduces psychiatric hospital contacts, physical health care use, and suicide attempts, but has no effect on labor market outcomes, including employment or disability pension. Still, savings from reduced use of other health care services exceed policy costs, making it both cost-reducing and welfare-improving." Read the full paper here: Benjamin Ly Serena (2025), The Causal Effect of Scaling up Access to Psychotherapy. Review of Economics and Statistics, forthcoming:  https://lnkd.in/eq3bX2JG

    Every woman deserves a safe pregnancy and childbirth experience, regardless of her income level or where she lives. Yet for many women, especially in underserved communities, access to quality maternal healthcare remains limited by cost and availability. Private sector health financing has an important role to play in closing this gap. By expanding health insurance coverage, introducing innovative and sustainable financing models, and partnering with the public sector, private actors can help make maternal healthcare more affordable, accessible, and responsive to women’s needs. The question is no longer whether the private sector has a role to play, but how effectively governments can create an enabling environment and work with private actors to build the conditions that make safe, affordable maternal care possible for all women. What actions are needed now to make this collaboration work? Let's get your views on this important issue as this collaboration can strengthen health systems, reduce preventable maternal deaths, and ensure that no woman is left behind at the moment she needs care most. #GivingBirthInNigeria #MaternalHealth

    The Revenue Leak Most Behavioral Health Practices Never See On paper, everything looks fine. The calendar is full, clients are showing up, and sessions are happening back-to-back. Yet, every month, the numbers feel slightly disappointing. It’s not alarming enough to panic—but just enough to wonder: “Am I missing something… or is this just how it is?” This is a common scenario. A therapist opens a practice to help people regulate emotions—not to navigate insurance portals. Billing gets set up, claims go out, and denials happen (because of course they do). Someone says, “We’ll follow up.” And then life takes over. Sessions. Notes. Supervision. Burnout. Denied claims sit quietly in a work queue, with money waiting patiently to expire. No urgency. No alerts. Just silence. The math most clinicians were never shown Imagine a clinician seeing 20–22 sessions per week, with an average allowed amount of ~$120–$140. That’s roughly $130,000–$140,000 annually per clinician. Now factor in: **7–10% initial denials (very common in behavioral health) **A few underpayments that go unnoticed **Follow-ups that don’t happen consistently That’s 15–18% revenue leakage. Not because of poor care. Not because of fraud. But because of process gaps. 👉 $20,000–$25,000 per clinician, per year—quietly disappearing. Why behavioral health billing feels heavier than other specialties Because it is. Codes look simple, but they aren’t. Telehealth rules change constantly. Payers apply “behavioral health logic” that isn’t written anywhere. One documentation nuance can invalidate weeks of claims. And there’s another layer no one talks about enough: Clinicians don’t like chasing money. It feels uncomfortable. Confrontational. Like it steals energy from patient care. So many practices adapt in the most human way possible: They stop looking too closely. The real issue isn’t reimbursement — it’s visibility Healthy billing isn’t exciting. It’s boring. Predictable. Quiet. You know what’s billed. What’s denied. What’s pending. And what’s genuinely uncollectible. When that clarity exists, billing stops taking emotional space. And emotional space is expensive. One honest question worth asking - If you stopped seeing new patients for 30 days… Would your existing billing pipeline still support you? If that question feels uncomfortable, you’re not alone. Final thought Money stress doesn’t make clinicians greedy. It makes them tired. And tired clinicians burn out faster. You don’t need to love billing. You just need it to stop competing for your attention. Because your best work happens when your mind isn’t decoding EOBs at midnight. #BehavioralHealth #MentalHealthProfessionals #PrivatePractice #TherapistsOfLinkedIn #HealthcareOperations #PracticeManagement #ClinicianBurnout #HealthcareFinance #RevenueCycle
